macro_segments.title: Salting as a mechanism of taste transfer: distinguishing between blood and non-kosher juices

micro_segments.title: The previous case of salting kosher and non-kosher meat together and the attempt to prove from Shmuel's statement
The distinction between salting and heat in terms of which object's status matters for taste transfer
Why the Braita cannot definitively prove whether both pieces being salted is permitted or forbidden
Reading the Braita to clarify that only when the non-kosher fish is salted does a problem arise
The conclusion that if both are salted, taste transfer occurs despite both spewing juices

macro_segments.title: The special principles governing salting for blood removal in koshering meat

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's statement that meat does not fully release all its blood and the necessity of proper salting and rinsing
The practical requirements of salting meat well and rinsing well, and the role of mimetic tradition
The debate between Rav Huna and the Baraita regarding the order of rinsing and salting
The eight reasons given by the Rishonim for why meat must be rinsed before salting
Rav Dimi's example of using thick salt and shaking it off, and Rav Mesharshaya's statement about blood in inner organs
Rashi's explanation of scattering salt and not reabsorbing blood, contrasted with the benefits of thick kosher salt
The requirement that salted meat be placed on a perforated vessel or slanted board
Rav Sheshet's practice of salting each piece individually and the Gemara's explanation that this is not necessary
Shmuel's prohibition against breaking an animal's neck before it dies to prevent blood from coming out
The dispute whether breaking the neck makes the meat unkosherable or merely constitutes robbery and blood absorption

macro_segments.title: The rabbinic restriction against having meat and cheese at the same table

micro_segments.title: The Mishna stating that placing poultry and cheese on the same table does not violate a biblical prohibition
The Gemara's attempt to read into the Mishna a possible tannaitic opinion that poultry is biblically prohibited

macro_segments.title: The biblical prohibition of cooking meat and milk together and its scope

micro_segments.title: The Tanna Kamma's statement that kosher meat of a kosher animal cooked with its milk is biblically forbidden
The permissibility of cooking non-kosher species meat or milk with kosher meat
The rabbinic distinction that while chicken and cheese cannot be at the same table, chicken and milk can be cooked together
The importance of the prohibition against deriving benefit from cooked meat and milk, including in dog food

macro_segments.title: Tannaitic debate on whether wild animals and birds are included in the meat and milk prohibition

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva's clarification that birds and wild animals are not biblically prohibited based on the phrase 'mother's milk'
Rabbi Yossi HaGelili's derivation that things prohibited as neveilah are included, but birds are excluded because they have no mother's milk

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's expansion of the meat and milk prohibition through drashas on the word 'gedi' (kid)

micro_segments.title: Establishing that 'gedi' does not refer only to a goat but to any young animal through the paradigm of 'gedi ha-izim'
Shmuel's three inclusive drashas: fat, dead animals, and fetuses are all included in the prohibition
Shmuel's three exclusive drashas: blood, placenta, and non-kosher animals are all excluded from the prohibition

macro_segments.title: The distinction between what constitutes 'meat' for the purposes of the meat and milk prohibition

micro_segments.title: The requirement in the Torah verse that the milk must be from the mother and must be milk of a living animal

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's expansion of the meat and milk prohibition through drashas on the word 'gedi' (kid) (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's resolution that Shmuel's six drashas are compressed into three by recognizing that some are already assumed or derived together
Shmuel's principle that one prohibition can take place on top of another (issur chal al issur), affecting how forbidden fat and neveilah are treated

macro_segments.title: The distinction between what constitutes 'meat' for the purposes of the meat and milk prohibition (continued)

micro_segments.title: The clarification that fat is considered meat, blood is not considered meat, and the placenta is not considered meat

topical_tags.term: eino ne'echal machmat milcho (so salty it cannot be eaten)
dog food meat and milk issue
lo tevashel gedi bachalav imo (do not cook a kid in its mother's milk)
issur chal al issur (one prohibition on top of another)
